# tooling

<!-- awf:edit current-state: from .awf/domains/parts/tooling/current-state.md -->
## Current state

ADR-0146 establishes the Go-owned workflow telemetry protocol, resident ledger boundary, canonical projection engine, diagnostic command surfaces, and descriptor projection consumed by Pi rendering. ADR-0149 advances the embedded descriptor to protocol 2.0 with closed privacy-minimal events and lifecycle requests, compatible-minor preservation, bounded identifiers, no repository-path field, the `provisional-overflow-resume` abandonment reason used when structured resume leaves a durably settled provisional effort, and a single transactional `phase_transitioned` chain edge. `internal/telemetry` provides confined atomic creation, leased durable append, causal discovery/route/phase/trajectory projection, idempotent transition handling, deterministic terminal-effort retention, selector-driven aggregation, normalized export, exact workflow violations, and comparable-route heuristics. ADR-0154 reconciles anchor semantics: anchor claims are owned exclusively by `trajectory_closed` events keyed on the payload anchor and declared in the required `anchorClaimKinds` descriptor vocabulary, the envelope `piAnchorId` is observation-location metadata the causal checker never reads, references resolve causally forward only, and anchor causal position never invalidates a session association. Every canonical finding names its owning effort, and repair or waiver requests require that finding's eligible reason, evidence, scope, and current nonempty frontier. The gated `awf metrics` family queries, exports, mutates lifecycle state, and performs confirmed maintenance; gated `awf doctor` is read-only and advisory. Neither surface produces a health score or automatically blocks, waives, repairs, or reconciles workflow state. Resident data excludes conversational content, tool arguments and output, raw commands, and every repository path; protocol-1 resident data is never rewritten or automatically deleted, and the threat model does not cover a hostile same-user process or provide cryptographic tamper evidence.

ADR-0150 adds the repository-only pinned dashboard runtime. `internal/dashboardruntime` resolves the local `refs/awf/dashboard-runtime` commit, builds awf and a closed launcher from an isolated materialization under a normalized Go environment, and atomically publishes an immutable content-addressed XDG cache entry with verified metadata and a complete canonical telemetry-policy snapshot. The private `dashboard-read` route bypasses live project version gating only after exact read-only argv, executable, snapshot, repository, protocol, and metrics-root validation; every mutation and maintenance path retains ordinary gates. `./x dashboard-awf-path` resolves the launcher, while reviewed `./x dashboard-awf-advance [commit]` publishes before compare-and-swap advancement.

ADR-0132 raises the config schema to generation 13, expands the curated default to twelve core skills, and gives Pi's exploration lane the exact required `{task, breadth, detail}` contract while retaining four public roles and the unchanged runner boundary. Schema generation 16 and awf 0.21.0 add the positive default-20 `currentState.maxClaimsPerTopic` setting; working-tree `awf check` emits one sorted, non-failing split note per topic strictly above it, while equality is quiet and `awf check --staged` suppresses the advisory.

Prose-gate evaluates staged configuration and regular staged blobs.

ADR-0124 makes sync tooling consume the deterministic project output plan: manifests contain planned writes once, pruning protects planned local reservations, and init collision checks receive the same write paths. Target capability and duplicate-target validation fail before any render or filesystem write.

ADR-0123 adds a mandatory Docker-backed Pi-extension lane to `./x gate`. A digest-pinned image and
repo/dependency-keyed persistent container snapshot the read-only checkout, type-check the actual
dogfooded TypeScript, and enforce 100% statement/branch/function/line coverage through c8 without
host Node/npm state; `./x pi-test run|stop|reset` owns its lifecycle. The lane type-checks and covers every governed extension file and runs an in-memory compatible fork Pi
0.81.1 runtime to prove partial details, result middleware, queued handoff settlement, cancellation, parent lineage, and truthful replacement-failure boundaries on the minimum runtime; incompatible-API fixtures prove the official-0.81.1-shaped surface fails closed before registration or pending state, alongside
bounded event, failure-diagnostic, cancellation, and cleanup tests. The runtime lane covers exact model routing, limiter lifecycle including setup failure, and whole-batch enforcement through an in-memory Pi session. ADR-0125 adds exact four-tool
schema and role checks plus context-isolation and shared-renderer state/width coverage at the same
minimum Pi version. ADR-0126 has the lane strip the `// @ts-nocheck` directive from its checkout
snapshot before `tsc`, so the type-check still covers the real extension code while adopter IDEs stay
quiet on the shipped files.

First adoption validates the brownfield ADR boundary before scaffold or stdout mutation, then seals initializedWithVersion, both ADR format cutoffs, and the gaps through an init-only project path. Schema-15 upgrade computes the existing V1 adopter's V2 cutoff through the ADR corpus and saves schema, version, and cutoff once. Ordinary sync requires and preserves permanent authority; upgrade routes bridge, permanent, pre-tracking, and invalid locks before mutation. The read-only `awf init --describe` descriptor query remains available in a pre-tracking tree while mutating and authority-consuming commands stay guarded.

Repo interactions split across two entry points (ADR-0156): the rendered `./awf` wrapper forwards every awf CLI verb verbatim through the from-source `awfInvokeCmd` (`go run ./cmd/awf`), while the hand-written `./x` project runner carries the repo-local development verbs; `./x sync` and `./x check` retain repository-special example-adopter work. The gate is `go test ./... && go vet && golangci-lint` plus a whole-program dead-code pass (`deadcode` without `-test`, piped through `cmd/deadcodecheck`; ADR-0063), with a hard 100% statement-coverage floor (`cmd/covercheck`; a genuinely-unreachable branch may carry a justified `// coverage-ignore:`). The dead-code gate fails on any production function unreachable from a `main` outside `internal/testsupport/` and carries no escape hatch. Separately, `./x mutants` (ADR-0066) is an advisory, never-gated mutation-testing command: it runs `gremlins` under a deterministic config (`.gremlins.yaml`; integration mode, one worker) over the production diff vs `main` or a given package, and `cmd/mutants` prints the survived (`LIVED`) mutants to triage; exiting non-zero when any mutant times out, since a timeout can hide a survivor (trustworthy only when the run reports `Timed out: 0`). `./x audit-local` (ADR-0073) runs `cmd/repoaudit`, standalone repo-local dev tooling deliberately outside the shipped standard: it mirrors `awf audit`'s finding contract (Warning/Error, non-zero exit only on an Error) over a required commit range with two rules: changelog conformance (an adopter-facing change in range with no `[Unreleased]` entry is an advisory Warning, ADR-0107, a path heuristic that informs without blocking; a git/read failure inside the rule stays an Error) and coverage-ignore re-evaluation (an added or touched `// coverage-ignore` in a production Go file is a Warning prompting a reachability re-check), and never runs the gate. golangci-lint is a pinned `go tool` dependency; awf never activates git hooks (ADR-0032) but renders inert payload scripts under `.awf/hooks/` when the `hooks` singleton is enabled (ADR-0048); this repo enables it, and the executable `.githooks/` stubs (wired once per clone with `git config core.hooksPath .githooks`) delegate to those payloads to run the gate on commit/push. awf ships as prebuilt cross-platform binaries built by GoReleaser on `v*` tag pushes (ADR-0030); `project.Version` is the single version authority (ADR-0049); it drives the CLI version, the lock, the bootstrap pin, and the version gate, the Release workflow refuses a tag that does not match it, `minVersionBySchema` ties schema-generation bumps to version bumps, and a PR-time `goreleaser check`/snapshot job guards the release config. ADR-0079 hardens the release path: every remote workflow action reference is SHA-pinned and the GoReleaser tool version exact (`cmd/pincheck`, run by `./x gate`, fails an unpinned ref, an undigested `docker://` ref, or a floated `version:` input, with weekly dependabot keeping the pins current) while `release.yml` refuses a tag whose commit is not on `origin/main` and runs `./x gate` and `./x check` before building; Codecov uploads skip without their token (coverage enforcement is the local gate), and the release checksum is documented as integrity-only, publisher compromise being an accepted, recorded risk. The changelog is pinned at release time, not per commit (ADR-0078): the every-commit gate holds only ordering (`TestChangelogMonotonicOrder`: entries strictly descending, newest at or below `project.Version`), so a mid-cycle version bump never touches the changelog, while `cmd/releasecheck`, run by the Release workflow before GoReleaser (a gate test asserts the wiring) and locally as the runbook's pre-tag rehearsal, refuses a release whose newest changelog entry is not exactly `project.Version` or whose standing `[Unreleased]` section is missing or non-empty. `cmd/releasecheck` also carries the `project.BridgeTrancheComplete` sentinel: Plans 1 and 2 of the current-state bridge are one unreleased v0.18.0 tranche, and the check refuses publication from any intermediate commit while the const is `false`, so the tranche can only be released once both plans have landed and flipped it `true`. The GitHub Release body is that same curated section, not a commit-derived changelog (ADR-0096): `.goreleaser.yaml` sets `changelog: disable: true`, and `release.yml` extracts the tagged version with `go run ./cmd/awf changelog --version "${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}"` into `$RUNNER_TEMP` (outside the worktree, so GoReleaser's dirty-tree check still passes) and passes it via `--release-notes`; a gate test mirroring the releasecheck wiring backs the invariant, so a commit subject can no longer leak into the release notes. The rendered bootstrap resolves an exactly-matching PATH `awf` before downloading and keeps stdout to the single resolved-path line.

awf is positioned as a tool-agnostic renderer (ADR-0016): adapter output paths (skills, agents, an optional bridge) come from a named `Target` rather than `.claude/` literals. A project selects one or more adapters through the `targets` array (default `[claude]`, ADR-0037), resolved against a built-in registry of adapters (`claude`, `codex`, `copilot`, `cursor`, `gemini`, `pi`), each rendering skills and agents into its native layout with an optional bridge file (`claude`/`gemini`). ADR-0122 grew that registry from two adapters to six and made agent output format target-owned: a target names its `AgentDialect` and `AgentSuffix`, so `codex` emits a TOML profile while the rest emit Markdown, and a target's declared `Capabilities` can vary skill content without a render-loop change. Pi additionally declares exactly five non-catalog outputs under `.pi/extensions/awf-subagents/`, `.pi/extensions/awf-handoff/`, and `.pi/extensions/awf-dashboard/`. Descriptor-derived protocol attribution, active association copying, lifecycle durability, canonical refresh, widget and overlay registration, shutdown drain, degraded behavior, cancellation, parent lineage, preserved history, kickoff, editor fallback, and the nontransactional replacement boundary are covered by the Pi runtime lane. Adapter artifacts render once per enabled target and a dropped target's whole tree is pruned. `awf init` pre-flights every path it would write and aborts on a collision with a pre-existing, non-managed file unless `--force` is passed. It scaffolds a curated workflow-core default (ADR-0022): only the twelve `core`-flagged workflow skills are enabled, alongside all agents; `workflow`/`doc-standard`/`agents-md-standard` are mandatory always-on singletons outside the toggleable `docs:` catalog and no longer scaffold-enabled docs, and no doc carries `core` any longer (ADR-0043); the remaining catalog skills are opt-in via the config arrays or `awf enable`. Init fills config values from catalog-declared descriptors (ADR-0029): it prompts on a TTY, prints the descriptors as JSON with `--describe` for agent-driven onboarding, and accepts `--set k=v` (repeatable) / `--answers FILE` non-interactively; the silent non-TTY path still seeds every var empty. Init also offers a full-deselectable catalog trim: the catalog `vars:` block declares two `multiselect` descriptors (`skills`, `docs`) whose options and pre-selected core are computed from the catalog, so an operator can deselect core or add opt-in targets interactively or via `--set skills=`/`--set docs=`; the selection replaces the enable array verbatim, mirroring `awf disable`. For safe adoption into a repo that already has its own workflow (ADR-0023), every awf write backs up a colliding *foreign* file before overwriting it: `awf sync` (ADR-0035) backs up any path present on disk but absent from the prior lock to a free `<path>.awf-bak[.N]` sibling and reports it, and `init` keeps only its pre-sync collision *refusal* (it still refuses without `--force`) while delegating the backup itself to the chained sync; so there is one `BackupFile` mechanism across both, with no double-backup. When the backed-up foreign file is the generated ADR index or a per-domain index, sync additionally prints a one-time ownership-takeover note so an adopter's competing generator surfaces. `awf uninstall` removes the generated footprint (lock-tracked files, empty dirs, and the lock) while leaving the authored `.awf/` config in place. It preserves and reports nonempty resident `.awf/metrics/` data plus the governed ignore file; only confirmed metrics purge owns recursive effort cleanup. awf never installs or activates git hooks (ADR-0032): activation is the adopter's own (`core.hooksPath` or `.git/hooks/`), while ADR-0048 supplies the rendered payload content those setups can delegate to. The audit's history and working-tree reads go through go-git, so awf (and its tests) need no host `git` binary. A working snapshot opened from a nested adopted project resolves the containing monorepo and reroots its tracked and nonignored paths to the project, excluding sibling content (nonignored per the repository's own ignore chain plus the injected global and system excludesfile patterns of `git.GlobalExcludePatterns`, shared with the audit's uncommitted-changes rule); snapshot marker loading also excludes deeper directories that carry their own `.awf/config.yaml`. `awf enable`/`disable`/`list` (ADR-0024) manage the four catalog/domain config enable arrays via a required `<kind>` token: `skill`/`agent`/`doc` against the catalog, `domain` as a freeform create validated by `config.ValidateDomainName`. The kind grammar (which enable array, catalog pool, and labels each token resolves to) comes from one ordered kind-descriptor table in `internal/project` shared with the render/check paths, not a per-command switch (ADR-0027). A fifth `target` token (ADR-0037) takes a bespoke path outside that table: it validates against the adapter registry and writes the whole resolved list via `config.SetArray`. Bespoke `bootstrap` (ADR-0040) and `hooks` (ADR-0048) tokens likewise stay outside the kind-descriptor table: each toggles its config-tree singleton, the self-pinning `.awf/bootstrap.sh` (relocated by ADR-0047) and the `.awf/hooks/` payload set, via a nested `<key>.enabled` scalar (`config.SetMappingScalar`, one shared `enableDisableSingleton` path). Array edits go through `config.SetArrayMember`, a comment-preserving `yaml.Node` round-trip that accepts flow- or block-style input (ADR-0026), rather than a two-space string editor. A binary-version gate (ADR-0039) precedes those reads: the gated commands (`sync`, `check`, `invariants`, `audit`, `metrics`, `doctor`, `list`, `config`, `context`, `topic`, `new`, `enable`, `disable`) refuse to run against a project rendered by a *newer* awf (schema generation ahead, or lock `awfVersion` ahead) and `awf check` prints a non-failing notice when the binary is ahead of the project. ADR-0076 narrows the gate's tolerance: a present-but-unreadable `awf.lock` is now a hard error (with a restore-or-delete recovery hint) rather than a skipped version sub-check, so a corrupt lock can no longer read as "current"; the surviving skip set is an absent lock, an absent or empty `awfVersion`, or a non-semver `awfVersion`. `awf upgrade` reports its edge states truthfully: "update your pinned awf" when the binary is behind the tree's schema, and "not an awf project (run `awf init`)" outside any config layout. Every project-requiring command gives the same init hint when `.awf/config.yaml` is missing.

ADR-0127 makes the audit range caller-supplied: `awf audit` takes a required positional (a bare `<base>` meaning `<base>..HEAD`, or `<a>..<b>`), the `audit.baseBranch` key and `--base` flag are gone behind a schema-11 migration, and every run reports the range and commit count it evaluated so a verdict never reads without its scope; a range resolving to zero commits announces itself instead of printing clean, staying exit-zero so ADR-0017's `audit-empty-range-clean` survives. `cmd/repoaudit` loses its `origin/main..HEAD` default on the same grounds. `awf audit` (ADR-0017) reports advisory workflow-conformance findings over an explicit commit range, wired into no gate. Its rules cover Conventional-Commits, ADR-status/INDEX.md co-change, an ADR-frontmatter-parse warning (`adr-frontmatter`, surfaced when a changed ADR's new frontmatter does not parse, so the status rules' skip is explained rather than silent), the `current-state-transition` check (every parent-to-commit snapshot pair in the range is evaluated, so a missing, mutated, or unmatched application batch is reported and a correct endpoint cannot rescue a bad intermediate pair), dependency-without-ADR, large-change-without-plan, and, for domain-doc currency (ADR-0019), `domain-doc-staleness` (an ADR reaching Implemented in a configured domain without its current-state narrative refreshed), `undocumented-domain` (an ADR tagged with a domain that has no domain doc), and `domain-code-staleness` (ADR-0077: in-range churn in a domain's sidecar-declared `paths` territory, anchored path globs in `.awf/domains/<name>.yaml`, matched via the shared `internal/pathglob` dialect with generated paths excluded, without a co-change to that domain's current-state narrative; inert for a domain without `paths`). A further advisory rule, `plain-punctuation` (ADR-0117), warns when a commit raises the count of a typographic punctuation substitute (the em-dash, en-dash, ellipsis, or a curly quote) in a non-generated markdown file under `docsDir`: the trigger is a net increase per commit per file, comparing the change's old and new text, so existing prose is grandfathered without an allowlist and a doc that legitimately depicts a glyph warns rather than blocks. Two blind spots are accepted rather than closed, both bounded by that advisory severity: a net-zero swap within one file is silent, and a renamed doc presents as an add (the range differ takes no rename detection), so moving a doc warns on its whole grandfathered content. One rule, `uncommitted-changes` (ADR-0025), is range-independent: it inspects the live working tree via go-git's `Worktree().Status()` (with global/system gitignore patterns injected so it mirrors `git status`) and reports a branch-level Error if the tree is not clean; caught at the reviewing-impl review step, which treats audit Errors as blocking. Each rule is independently disable-able via `audit` config.

`awf commit-gate` (ADR-0036) is the deterministic, blocking counterpart to the advisory audit: it validates a single commit message against the Conventional Commits rule at commit time and exits non-zero on a violation, so a bad subject is refused rather than only reported later. The rule has one definition: the per-commit `audit.CheckConventionalCommit` is consumed by both the `awf audit` range loop and `commit-gate`, sourcing the same `audit.Resolve` settings (allowed types/scopes, 72-char subject), so the two can never drift. `commit-gate` reads the message a `commit-msg` hook passes as `$1` (or stdin), cleans it git-style (strips comment lines, stops at a verbose scissors line), and exempts git-generated merge/autosquash subjects. awf renders the `commit-msg.sh` payload but never wires it (ADR-0048); the adopter's own `commit-msg` hook does (this repo's checked-in `.githooks/commit-msg` stub delegates to the payload, which runs `./awf commit-gate "$1"`). `awf prose-gate` (ADR-0119) is the presence-level blocking counterpart to the advisory `plain-punctuation` rule: it scans every tracked text file (`internal/prosegate` over `git.IndexPaths`) for the seven banned typographic substitutes and exits non-zero on any finding outside the pinned `proseGate.exemptions`, answering whether the tree is clean rather than whether a commit made it worse. It is opt-in and default-off (a presence gate would fail an unswept adopter's build on the day it lands), self-gates on `proseGate.enabled` so a hook may invoke it unconditionally, is `Ungated` like `commit-gate` (a hook-wired check must not refuse on binary-version skew), and refuses rather than degrading to a filesystem walk outside a git repository. awf enables it for itself and wires it into `./x gate`; the rendered pre-commit payload gains an `awf prose-gate` line, so this repo scans twice (once via the payload's `./x gate`, once via its own line) by accepted design.

`awf changelog` (ADR-0041) prints the hand-maintained, embedded `changelog/CHANGELOG.md`: entries grouped per release into Breaking changes/Features/Bug fixes/Others by adopter-facing effect, not by mirroring a commit's Conventional-Commits type; the GitHub Release notes are generated from this file (ADR-0096), not re-derived from commit subjects. With no flags it prints the whole file; `--version <v>` prints one release, `--since <v>` prints every release after `v` (exclusive), and `--range <from>..<to>` prints an inclusive span; the three flags are mutually exclusive. `internal/changelog` parses the embedded markdown (mirroring `internal/catalog`'s `templates.FS` pattern) and implements the three filters; a new top-level `changelog/` package holds the raw file since `go:embed` cannot cross a package's own directory boundary.

`awf new topic <domain> "<title>"` scaffolds the two current-state topic inputs without
syncing. It validates a configured canonical domain, derives a collision-free kebab slug while
reserving `index` and refusing orphaned halves, writes metadata under `.awf/topics/metadata/` and an
empty-claim authored part under `.awf/topics/parts/`, and prints both repository-relative paths. The
metadata carries a valid anchored path placeholder and the part carries generic editable prose plus
an empty final `## Claims`; adopters must edit paths and prose and author reviewed claims manually.
The command mutates no config, lock, or rendered doc, and rolls its first file back if the second
write fails. A zero-claim shell can render but does not satisfy scoped coverage. `awf topic` accepts
exactly one `<domain>/<topic>` or `<domain>/<topic>:<claim>` selector and uses one deterministic model
for human and `--json` output. Defaults show active title/summary, claims, types, prose, and backing
while hiding provenance and references. Independent `--history`, `--references`, and `--coverage`
flags add only direct ADR operations, direct incoming/outgoing edges, and declared/effective scope plus
configured marker sites. A removed claim identity resolves only with `--history` as historical-only
Origin/Revised-by/Removed-by operations, an empty active-claim projection, and no former prose,
references, coverage, or tombstone object. A valid legacy-baseline removal explicitly reports that
its origin is not retained in active authority (`legacyBaseline: true` in JSON, with no guessed
`origin`). Queries never traverse references or write the worktree,
index, config, or lock; outside an adopted tree the command prints a static reference before gating.
`awf upgrade` migrates the config tree to the current schema and syncs; when the lock carries a bridge
attestation it instead performs the final current-state cutover. It verifies only the sealed facts (the
prepared HEAD and a tree digest over the post-normalization config, domains, ADRs, topics, and marker
sources), then drives one journaled transaction recorded at `.awf/current-state-upgrade.journal`: it
deletes the migration approval file and replaces the lock last with the attestation cleared and the
permanent ADR format cutoff and gaps promoted, running no project tests or gate. `awf upgrade --recover`
replays the journal recovery table, rolling an interrupted cutover back to its prior images or cleaning
up a committed one, idempotently. A central command-state guard in `cmd/awf` enforces the transaction
matrix before gating: a committed journal permits only `awf upgrade --recover`; a committed attestation
permits only plain `awf upgrade`, which consumes it; a malformed journal refuses every mode with
deterministic Git-restoration-and-bridge-reinstallation guidance; a corrupt lock without a journal keeps
the existing ADR-0076 refusal; and `awf version`, `awf changelog`, and `awf help` always bypass the
transaction state. Attestation and readiness reporting live only in the preceding bridge release; this
binary consumes seals through `internal/upgrade`, it never produces them.

`awf new adr "<title>"` (ADR-0042) scaffolds a new ADR file: `internal/adr.NextNumber` computes the next sequential number and `internal/adr.NewFile` copies the rendered `docs/decisions/template.md`, strips its marker comments, and fills in the date and title heading, replacing the free-hand copy/strip/number steps `awf-proposing-adr` used to spell out by hand. ADR-0068 widens `awf new` to project-local artifacts: `awf new skill <name> "<description>"` / `awf new agent <name> "<description>"` validates the kebab-case name (`config.ValidateArtifactName`), writes a declaring sidecar plus a starter `content` part, enables the name in the matching config array, and re-syncs, so a project adds its own skills and agents without forking the catalog. The scaffolded starter part opens with the whole-line `<!-- awf:stub -->` marker (ADR-0070), so `awf check` reports the artifact as unauthored until the author deletes the line. All `awf new` forms go through the ADR-0039 binary-version gate like the other project-reading commands.

`internal/testsupport` (ADR-0044) is awf's shared test-fixture package: `TestMain` HOME isolation, project-config scaffolding, ADR frontmatter fixtures, file-writing primitives, and the seam-swap idiom, plus `internal/testsupport/gitfixture` for git-repo fixtures, consolidating idioms that had drifted into independent, sometimes-inconsistent copies across `cmd/awf`, `internal/project`, `internal/audit`, `internal/coverage`, and `internal/migrate`. ADR-0144 gives the executable test-support tree direct tooling/test-infrastructure ownership and removes it from `contextIgnore`. It remains a leaf package: no file in `internal/testsupport` (including `gitfixture`) may import another `internal/*` awf package, mechanically enforced by a dedicated test that walks the package's own import graph, while standard-library imports and go-git inside `gitfixture` remain permitted.

`internal/evals` (ADR-0053) is a test-only package that renders the full catalog via `Project.SyncReport` and asserts cross-artifact workflow-chain seams no single-template test covers. ADR-0054 hardens this into a machine-enforced handoff convention: the ten chain-progression skills share the `terminal-step` handoff marker, each forward handoff must name its successor on an invocation-verb line, and the chain graph must stay connected (no orphaned node, every node reachable from `brainstorming`, with `retrospective` the sole terminal since ADR-0067); a companion section-parity guard (`TestSkillAndAgentSectionParity`, invariant `skill-section-parity`) asserts every skill/agent template's `awf:section` markers match its catalog-declared sections, so a section-slug rename cannot half-land with a blank-path provenance pointer. ADR-0069 adds a working-memory coverage lock (`memory-checkpoint-chain-coverage`): the nine non-terminal chain-node skills plus `bugfix` and `debugging` must render the checkpoint instruction in the full-catalog output, and `retrospective` the deletion step. ADR-0074 makes the three chain review subagents report-only judges: the shared review-discipline spine and their templates instruct the agent to report findings, never to edit, commit, or re-review, and each `<prefix>-reviewing-*` skill owns fix application and a single fresh verify-pass dispatch (invariant `reviewers-report-only`).

`awf enable`/`awf disable` are dependency-graph-aware (ADR-0081): a resolver computes provenance-carrying plans over the catalog `requires*` edges: enable brings in the full missing forward closure in one config rewrite, disable prints the dependent plan and refuses while it exceeds the target (`--with-dependents` cascades, `--dry-run` previews either plan), the CLI's first flags, parsed once by the generic driver (ADR-0094) that dispatches every command from the declarative `internal/clispec` command table, with `enable`/`disable` sharing a single direction-parameterised handler. `awf config [<key-or-var>]` (ADR-0088) prints the configuration reference from the CLI: the full reference or a single entry (a config key path, a var name, a sidecar field, or a data key name; an unknown token is an exit-1 error naming the bare form). Inside an adopted tree it is a gated command sharing the doc's describe-model builder (`project.ConfigReferenceModel`), so current values, per-var consumers, and dormant hints match the generated doc by construction; outside one it degrades to a static catalog-wide reference (`config-command-static-fallback`) built from configspec plus `project.PotentialVarConsumers`, serving pre-adoption discovery instead of refusing.

ADR-0090 adds the committed example adopter `examples/sundial/` (its own Go module, invisible to every `./...` sweep) as the repo's worked example and rendered-output quality oracle: `./x sync` re-renders it with a source-built binary after the repo's own sync, and `./x check` additionally runs the example's `awf check` (failing on drift or any `note: ` line; the model adopter is smell-free), `awf invariants`, and `go test ./...`. `internal/project/example_wiring_test.go` pins the wiring; `awf audit` deliberately never runs there (no `.git` at the example root).

`awf new doc <name> "<description>"` (ADR-0091) rounds out the `awf new` scaffolder (its kind set is now `{adr, skill, agent, doc}`) scaffolding a project-local doc the way `awf new skill|agent` scaffold their kinds: a declaring `.awf/docs/<name>.yaml` sidecar carrying a name-derived title and the description, a stub `content` convention part (its doc-standard pointer prose, not a dead-linkable relative link), the `docs:` enable, and a re-render. The name may be path-aware (nested, e.g. `guides/ci`), validated through `config.ValidateDocName`, and a collision with a catalog doc name is refused upfront.

ADR-0144 now gives context its path-attributed foundation: normalized request queries retain directory or Git-selection status and sorted effective descendants, while each unique effective path points back to every selecting request. One working or index snapshot supplies path bytes, config, lock, topics, markers, nested-adopter boundaries, output declarations, classification, and authority. Primary classification follows the fixed outside-repository, nested-adopter, generated-output, symlink, context-ignored, not-found, covered, eligible-unowned precedence; safe ignored and generated paths still receive domain/topic authority, with one guard: a user-typed (never Git-selected) query containing glob metacharacters that resolves to a missing or context-ignored path carries a globLiteral flag, receives no domain or topic attribution (its string would otherwise falsely match selector globs), feeds no invocation-level topic entry, and renders an explicit globs-are-not-expanded hint. Symlinks remain inert target bytes and every parser excludes them. Known artifacts receive closed-role causal source/output attribution from reader-injected output declarations and deterministic role-specific navigation from loaded layout, config, declaration, topic, and ADR authorities. Topic and context coverage now share separate domain/topic selectors, the requirement that both match, concrete current matched paths, and marker sites rather than Cartesian effective pairs.

`awf context <path>...` (ADR-0092, retargeted by ADR-0134, projected for agents by ADR-0144, and grouped by topic by ADR-0147) is a read-only orientation oracle. It renders each applicable topic exactly once per invocation: selectors with a matched-path count and `--coverage` drilldown, the uncapped claim-ID roster, and, in the concise projection, the full detail of exactly the claims selected directly by an exact-path `state:`, `touches-state:`, or invariant proof marker across the invocation's effective paths, with a detail-omission line naming the drilldown when any rostered claim's detail is not shown. Effective paths are classified before authority and carry attribution: owning domains, applicable topic IDs, and their own direct-claim IDs. `awf context --full` uses the same semantic result to render every applicable current claim's backing and `Verify` contracts, state/touches/proof sites, sorted direct incoming and outgoing reference IDs, and Remaining operations once per topic, without recursively expanding referenced bodies or unrelated ADR history. Human and JSON renderers consume that selected projection; concise JSON omits the `full` key entirely, and JSON exists for machine consumption while agents read the text form. Managed complete-authority callers (the reviewer dispatches, plan resync included, and the ADR lifecycle) instruct `--full` runs whose arguments the dispatching parent resolves, implementer skills ground concisely and drill down on demand, and a parent pastes packet output into a child brief only when it already holds that output for its own purposes (ADR-0155). Two omissions are deliberate: the ADR reviewer receives no packet, because an explicit ADR path reports lifecycle progress rather than path-claim grounding (its reviewer queries destination topics via `awf topic`), and the generic exploration skill stays grounding-free because its target paths are unknown up front.

An explicit governed ADR path additionally reports its parsed number, title, lifecycle status, mutable-or-frozen state, and the warning that ADR prose is pending intent or decision history rather than current authority. Declaration-ordered operations use the ADR corpus's canonical application records to render exactly `proposed`, `remaining`, `applied`, or `canceled`; applied operations carry their state sequence and abandonment never cancels an applied effect. Claim state is exactly `active-current`, `historically-removed`, or `not-yet-current`. Full ADR projection adds only operation-linked current/historical claim provenance, backing, and marker sites, never plans, tags, relations, unrelated ADRs, prose-derived authority, or invented tombstones.

Working assembly uses `ContextFor`; staged assembly enters through `StagedContextRoot`, which loads project presence, config, lock, topics, markers, directory descendants, gating inputs, and eligibility from one immutable index snapshot without consulting dirty working config or lock bytes. The command writes nothing, is gated inside a tree, and both projections degrade to the same static notice outside one, stating that live classification and authority require adoption. `--range <a>..<b>` selects changed requests against the one working universe. `awf context --uncovered` retains its existing eligible-path coverage report and staged behavior; it rejects `--full` because coverage gaps are not an authority projection. A topic from one owner never satisfies another owner's gap and a global topic never satisfies scoped coverage. Each collapsed unowned entry is structured: directory entries (and a root collapse) carry the in-scope unowned-file count and the count of coverage-excluded files beneath them, so a mostly-generated directory never reads as wholly unowned; only counts render, never the excluded paths. The concise context text render prints each domain group's selector block once as a named Domain-paths header instead of repeating it per topic; JSON is unchanged.
